personaChecks.evidence: Front matter declares 'persona: developer'. The page is an API reference for the C# sealed class ContentAccessSettings — documenting class declaration, properties, static factory methods, equality operators, parameter tables, and exception behavior. This is purely developer-facing content with no admin-operational, business-strategic, or architect-level concerns. Consistent with the task definition's targetPersonas: ['developer'].

Evaluated whether additional personas are warranted: (1) Admin — no operational/configuration procedures; all interactions are through C# code, not the admin UI. Not applicable. (2) Business — no outcome-focused or strategic framing; content is implementation-level API documentation. Not applicable. (3) Architect — the breaking change section could surface at architecture review, but the content is implementation-migration detail (struct-to-class, default value changes), not system-design or integration-pattern discussion. Sibling reference pages (reference-content-item-query: developer only; reference-content-retriever-api: developer+architect) show architect is added only when content covers retrieval patterns and integration. Not needed here. Developer-only is correct.

Content uses exact API terminology throughout: 'sealed class', 'IEquatable<ContentAccessSettings>', 'operator overloads', 'IReadOnlyCollection<int>', 'ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull', 'init accessor'. Code examples are C#-first with inline comments showing property state ('// settings.IsSecured == false'). Assumes familiarity with .NET concepts (struct vs class semantics, reference types, boxing, operator overloading) without explaining them. Migration example shows before/after code patterns. This is precisely the right tone for developer reference material.

Depth is well-calibrated for a developer API reference: (1) Class declaration with namespace — gives the developer the exact type to import. (2) Properties documented with types, access level, and behavioral notes per factory method — complete lookup information. (3) Factory methods with parameter tables, exception tables, and remarks — comprehensive reference. (4) Code examples in each section demonstrate concrete usage, not just abstract descriptions. (5) Breaking change section includes before/after migration code — actionable. (6) Not too deep: does not expose internal implementation (no source code of the class itself, no discussion of how deduplication works internally). The page serves as a complete, self-contained reference that a developer can use without needing to read the source code.

Vocabulary is consistently developer-appropriate: 'static factory methods', 'sealed class', 'value-type semantics', 'stack allocation', 'deduplicated', 'read-only collection', 'fluent API'. No business/marketing terminology that would confuse developers. The term 'member roles' is used correctly per INV-content-030 — 'member' refers to registered visitors without admin access, and the page links to the member_roles_xp page for context. 'Visitor' is used correctly for anonymous website users ('accessible by all visitors without authentication').

classificationCheck.evidence: Title follows 'Reference - ContentAccessSettings' pattern per INV-structure-019. Content is structured API reference: class declaration, properties with type tables, method signatures with parameter/exception tables, comparison table, and equality operator documentation. While it includes usage examples and migration guidance, these serve the reference purpose — showing how to use the documented API elements. No step-by-step tutorial flow or goal-oriented how-to structure. Correctly classified as reference.

invariantResults.evidence: Persona value 'developer' is in the valid set: developer, admin, business, architect, all.

Developer/admin content rules followed: uses developer terminology ('sealed class', 'operator overloads', 'IEquatable') appropriately. Does not over-simplify for business audiences. Steps in usage examples are appropriately combined for developer readers.

Vocabulary is adjusted specifically to the developer audience. No business-user terminology used. The term 'member roles' bridges developer and product terminology — correctly linked to member_roles_xp for developer context.

Not directly applicable (page is reference, not step-by-step). However, the usage examples section appropriately presents each example as a self-contained block without unnecessary granularity — consistent with developer-audience step-merging allowance.

Page assumes basic C# and .NET familiarity: does not explain what a sealed class is, what operator overloading means, or how IEquatable works. Explains only Xperience-specific concepts (the three-tier access model, factory method behavior). Appropriately avoids over-explaining common developer concepts.

User type terminology is correct throughout: 'member' for registered visitors ('authenticated member', 'member roles', 'member role IDs'), 'visitor' for anonymous users ('all visitors without authentication'). No confusion between contact/visitor/customer.
Business user rules — page targets developer persona only. Business tone/screenshot requirements do not apply.
